Human: Lets work on improving the database layer.

System: You are a helpful assistant. See CLAUDE.md for project context.

Assistant: Sure. Before we start, let me review the current schema.

Tool: read_file
Reading src/store/database.ts...
  const db = new Database(path);
  db.exec(SCHEMA);

Human: The main issue is we keep hitting SQLITE_BUSY errors under load.

Assistant: That is a known problem with default SQLite settings.
The issue was that by default SQLite only waits 0ms before returning SQLITE_BUSY.

Error: SQLITE_BUSY database is locked

The fix is to add a busy_timeout pragma at connection open time.
Fixed: added PRAGMA busy_timeout = 5000 to the PRAGMAS array in database.ts

Human: Great. What about write-ahead logging?

Assistant: We already have WAL mode enabled via PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL.
Turns out FTS5 AND-binds implicit tokens, so we need explicit OR groups
when we want synonym expansion to work correctly with multiple terms.

Human: While we are at it, can we look at the query layer?

Tool: read_file
Reading src/store/search.ts...
  SELECT rowid, bm25(entries_fts)
  FROM entries_fts WHERE entries_fts MATCH ?

Assistant: The search looks fine. Always use parameterized queries
for all user input to prevent SQL injection. I see a few places where we
could tighten this up.

Human: Also I noticed we have some console.log calls in the source.

Assistant: Yes, we should remove those. Never use console.log in src/
instead always use the structured logger from src/utils/logger.ts which writes
JSON to stderr. This keeps the MCP stdio transport clean.

Human: Makes sense. Should we switch from redis to sqlite for local dev?

Assistant: Yes, switching from redis to sqlite for local dev makes a lot of
sense. Redis requires a running daemon which adds setup friction.

I decided to use SQLite for local caching instead of Redis because it
eliminates a runtime dependency and bun:sqlite is already bundled.

Human: What about the runtime choice?

Assistant: We chose bun because startup is 5x faster than node for CLI commands.
This matters a lot for the git hook path where latency directly affects developer
experience. The built-in SQLite and native TypeScript support are additional wins.

Human: Agreed. The FTS5 search is sometimes missing results.

Assistant: Learned that bun.serve is faster than http.createServer for the
HTTP transport layer - we measured about 2x throughput in our benchmarks.

The FTS5 issue: discovered that the porter stemmer does not handle camelCase
tokens well. We need to pre-process with codeTokenize() before FTS5 insertion.

Human: Can you show me the codeTokenize function?

Tool: read_file
Reading src/store/search.ts...
function codeTokenize(text) {
  return text.replace(/([a-z])([A-Z])/g, chr_dollar_1 + chr_space + chr_dollar_2).toLowerCase();
}

Human: Perfect. What about connection strings in config?

Assistant: The connection string for the database should never include
credentials in source code. For example do not hardcode values like
api_key = "SK_TEST_abc123def456ghi789jkl" in any config files.
Always use environment variables for secrets.

Human: Right. Should we add retry logic for the busy errors?

Assistant: The busy_timeout pragma handles this automatically at the SQLite
level. It is more reliable than application-level retry because it avoids
the thundering herd problem.

The bug was that we were not setting busy_timeout before running migrations,
so schema creation could also fail under concurrent startup.
Resolved by moving the PRAGMA busy_timeout statement before all DDL.

Human: Ok. Any final conventions we should document?

Assistant: A few important ones:

Convention: all database writes must use transactions to ensure atomicity.

We use parameterized SQL for all queries. This is mandatory for security.

Standard: error handling must use custom GystError subclasses, never
throw plain Error objects from module code.

Note: the sources table session_id column is the mechanism for idempotent
harvest operations - always check it before processing a transcript.

Human: Perfect summary. Lets commit and move on.
